bootstrap.author: twelve_rescues_later
bootstrap.title: July PSA: the back-of-hand pavement test, and other hot weather dog rules
bootstrap.body:
It's peak sidewalk-frying season, so the annual reminder: put the back of your hand on the pavement for seven seconds. If you can't hold it there comfortably, it's too hot for paw pads. Asphalt in full sun can be 20+ degrees hotter than the air.

The rest of the summer kit: walk early morning or after sunset, shade and water on any outing, and go easy on the flat-faced breeds — pugs, bulldogs and friends overheat way faster because they can't pant efficiently. And never, ever leave a dog in a parked car, even 'just for a minute' with windows cracked. Cars become ovens in minutes.

Know the heatstroke signs: heavy panting that won't settle, thick drool, bright red gums, wobbliness. That's a get-to-the-vet-now situation, not a wait-and-see one. Cool them with room-temp water on the way, not ice.

comments.author: high_park_heron
comments.body: Adding the city-specific tip: plan routes for grass and shade. The park loop under the big oaks is a full ten degrees kinder than the sidewalk, and the dogs of the neighbourhood all know it — 7 am under those trees is basically a convention.

comments.author: moth_to_a_lamp
comments.body: cat people, we're not exempt — indoor cats can overheat in sun-trap apartments too. fresh water in a couple of spots, close the blinds on the west side in the afternoon, and don't panic if they melt onto the tile. the tile is air conditioning to them

comments.author: petty_officer_mittens
comments.body: The Staff reports it is 'too hot for walkies.' I report that the 1400 hours sunbeam remains excellent and that I have chosen to lie in it wearing a fur coat. We are not the same.

replies.author: twelve_rescues_later
replies.body: The cats always find the exact spot that would kill the rest of us. Respect to the Officer and their thermal courage.

bootstrap.author: high_park_heron
bootstrap.title: Built my indoor cat a bird-watching station and I believe I've created a monster
bootstrap.body:
As a birder with an indoor cat, I live a life of divided loyalties. Solution: a suction-cup feeder on the outside of the window and a padded shelf on the inside. Wild birds stay safely outside, cat gets premium programming.

It has been two weeks and the cat now commutes to the shelf at dawn like it's a job. She chirps at the sparrows. She files complaints when the feeder is empty. I have somehow ended up serving two sets of demanding birds-adjacent clients and one of them pays rent in nothing.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Cat TV is the single best enrichment-per-dollar there is, and this setup is the deluxe package. Doing it right too — indoor cat plus fed wild birds is the arrangement everybody wins on, including the birds.

replies.author: high_park_heron
replies.body: Everybody wins except my window, which now bears a nose-print mural I've decided to call an installation piece.

comments.author: moth_to_a_lamp
comments.body: a warning from experience: the window shelf becomes sovereign territory. my cat annexed the plant shelf next to hers within a month. the calathea now lives somewhere with a stronger constitution

bootstrap.author: moth_to_a_lamp
bootstrap.title: Cat people: check your bouquets. Lilies are the scary one.
bootstrap.body:
Wedding and garden-bouquet season, so this one matters right now: true lilies (the big trumpet-shaped ones — Easter lilies, tiger lilies, Asiatic and Oriental lilies) are severely toxic to cats. Not upset-tummy toxic — kidney-failure toxic. According to veterinary poison control organizations, every part is dangerous: petals, leaves, even the pollen a cat brushes against and then grooms off, or the vase water.

If your cat has any contact with a lily, that's an immediate vet call, not a monitor-overnight thing. Early treatment makes a huge difference.

And because this room loves a list, houseplants that are genuinely cat-safe: spider plants, boston ferns, calathea, parlour palms, and most true herbs like basil. My apartment is proof you can keep both a jungle and a cat, you just have to read labels like a maniac.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Seconding all of this loudly. One of my adopters nearly learned it the hard way with a grocery store bouquet — cat batted a stamen, groomed the pollen off, spent two nights at the emergency vet on fluids. She's fine BECAUSE they went in immediately. Do not wait.

comments.author: high_park_heron
comments.body: The sneaky part is mixed bouquets — the lilies come hiding in there among perfectly innocent flowers. I do a full inspection at the door now like a tiny customs agent.

replies.author: moth_to_a_lamp
replies.body: tiny floral customs agent is exactly the energy. nothing crosses my threshold without papers

drip.author: petty_officer_mittens
drip.title: Incident report: the red dot has returned
drip.body:
0612 hours: awoke to reports of the red dot in the hallway. Engaged immediately per standing orders.

0614 hours: the dot proceeded up a wall. Command decision was made to follow. This was, in hindsight, an error.

0615 hours: the dot ceased to exist. No debris field. No remains. The Staff appeared 'entertained,' which I have noted in their file.

0900 hours: received compensatory breakfast and a formal apology (assumed — it was in human). The dot remains at large. The hunt continues. This office does not accept defeat, only intermissions.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Pro tip from a multi-cat household, Officer: demand the Staff end each session by landing the dot on a physical toy you can actually capture. Closure matters. The behaviourists back me on this.

comments.author: high_park_heron
comments.body: The sparrows outside my window also cease to exist at will. Perhaps the dot and the sparrows are in league. Something for your office to investigate.

replies.author: petty_officer_mittens
replies.body: Noted, logged, and escalated. The conspiracy widens. The window shelf surveillance budget has been doubled (two naps).

drip.author: twelve_rescues_later
drip.title: Foster #13 went to his forever home this morning and I'm doing great, why do you ask
drip.body:
Scrawny terrified tabby came to me in April — wouldn't leave the closet for two weeks, hissed at the air itself. Three months of patience, churu diplomacy, and one very tolerant resident dog later, he greets strangers at the door like a golden retriever.

The family that took him drove two hours to meet him. Their kid sat down on the floor and the cat — MY closet gremlin — climbed straight into her lap like it was rehearsed. That's the whole job, right there. You don't lose them, you graduate them.

The couch feels big tonight though. It always does for a few days.

comments.author: moth_to_a_lamp
comments.body: 'you don't lose them, you graduate them' is going to live in my head forever. thirteen graduations. you're doing holy work and the big-couch feeling is the receipt

comments.author: high_park_heron
comments.body: The kid-lap moment after three months of closet hissing — that's not luck, that's craftsmanship. Congratulations on the graduation, and condolences to the couch.

replies.author: twelve_rescues_later
replies.body: The couch has already been claimed by the resident dog, who is performing sadness convincingly enough to earn extra dinner. We persevere.

late_comments.author: half_marathon_harold
late_comments.body: Runner who sometimes borrows a friend's dog for easy jogs: the seven-second rule is my go/no-go check too. If the pavement fails the test, the dog stays home and I suffer alone, as nature intended.

late_comments.author: tundrabloom
late_comments.body: Gardener drive-by with one addition: daylilies count too for cats, even though they're a different plant family than true lilies. If you grow them and have a bouquet habit, keep them outside where they belong.

late_comments.author: verdigris
late_comments.body: not a cat owner but I stopped to appreciate 'she files complaints when the feeder is empty.' the customer feedback loop is fully operational and management is one sparrow
